title: The God-Kings and the Dynasty of Menelik
subtitle: 5880 BC — The Divine Dynasty and the Solomonic Connection

sections.title: The Era of God-Kings
sections.body:
Following the Great Flood, the 1922 Ethiopian Regnal List introduces one of its most fascinating chapters: the era of the 'God-Kings or Divine Dynasty,' which ruled from approximately 5880 to 5500 BC. This post-diluvian dynasty represents a transitional period between the legendary pre-Flood world and the more historically grounded eras that would follow.

The God-Kings are characterized by names that evoke divine and celestial associations, reflecting a worldview in which the boundary between mortal rulers and divine beings was fluid. The rulers of this dynasty were seen not merely as kings but as semi-divine figures who mediated between the human and spiritual realms — a concept that finds parallels in ancient Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and other Near Eastern traditions.

sections.title: The Warrior Queens of the Divine Dynasty
sections.body:
Notably, the era of God-Kings features several powerful female rulers. Queen Nehasset Nais, Queen Hathor, and Queen Ankh each held positions of supreme authority during this legendary period. Their inclusion in the Regnal List is significant — it demonstrates that Ethiopian tradition has long recognized women as capable sovereigns, a tradition that would continue with the historical Kandakes (queens) of Kush and the famous Queen of Sheba.

These warrior queens are described as wise and formidable rulers who commanded armies, built monuments, and maintained the spiritual traditions of their ancestors. Their reigns represent a golden age of female leadership in Ethiopian memory.

sections.title: The Dynasty of Menelik I
sections.body:
Perhaps the most significant section of the 1922 Regnal List is the 'Dynasty of Menelik I' — a lineage of paramount importance in Ethiopian history as it connects the legendary origins of the nation to the historically attested Solomonic dynasty. Menelik I, the son of King Solomon of Israel and the Queen of Sheba, is traditionally credited with bringing the Ark of the Covenant to Ethiopia.

The Regnal List provides an extensive chronology of monarchs who ruled after Menelik I, spanning nearly two millennia from the 10th century BC to the 10th century AD. This includes rulers with names like Amen Hotep, Aksumay I, and Abralyus, as well as a series of queens known as Kandakes (Kandake II, III, and IV), each ruling for periods of ten years. The inclusion of Kandakes reflects the historical reality of powerful female rulers in the ancient kingdom of Kush.

This elaborate genealogy serves a crucial political and religious purpose: it legitimizes the Solomonic dynasty's claim to an ancient, divinely ordained lineage tracing back to King Solomon himself — and through him, to the biblical patriarchs and the founding of the world.

sections.title: The Ankh: Symbol of Ethiopian Sovereignty
sections.body:
Throughout Ethiopian legendary history, one symbol recurs with profound significance: the Ankh. In the traditional narrative, the Ankh was the 'great Stave of Ethiel' — the mark of the King of Kings that signified supreme authority. Ethiopian tradition boldly claims that this symbol originated in Ethiopia and spread to Egypt and Sudan through the descendants of Gera, rather than being borrowed from Egyptian culture.

The Ankh appears in Ethiopian Christian art, royal regalia, and manuscript illuminations throughout the medieval period. Its presence in Ethiopian tradition represents a fascinating example of cultural syncretism — the adoption and reinterpretation of an ancient symbol that connected Ethiopian Christianity to the deep past of the Nile Valley civilizations. Whether the Ankh originated in Ethiopia or was adopted from Egypt, its enduring presence in Ethiopian royal iconography testifies to the profound cultural connections between the Horn of Africa and the ancient Nile Valley.

The Regnal List is not merely a collection of names and dates; it is a narrative framework that connects Ethiopia's origins to the broader history of the ancient Near East, establishing a direct link between the biblical patriarchs and the founding of the Ethiopian nation.
